summary: Joel analyzes Ipswitch's failed pricing strategy for their IMail email server, where the company tried to move upmarket to compete with Microsoft Exchange by adding collaboration features and raising prices. He argues they made two fundamental mistakes: underestimating Microsoft's ability to charge more simply by being the market leader, and misframing the value equation by hoping customers would compare their new prices to Exchange rather than to their own previous pricing. Joel uses this as a case study in software pricing psychology, explaining that perceived value is always relative to a baseline comparison. He concludes that small companies should never compete on price against larger rivals who have inherent cost advantages.
key_points: Microsoft can charge more for equivalent software because market leadership lets them capture more consumer surplus through brand trust and a massive sales force
Customers judge new prices against the most natural baseline — usually the previous version's price — not against a competitor the vendor wishes they'd compare to
Ipswitch confused two different markets (large corporate email vs. small ISPs and businesses) that have very different willingness to pay
Small companies should never compete on price against larger rivals who can distribute development costs over more customers and already have sunk marketing costs
Premium-priced 'decoy' editions (like Visual Studio Enterprise Architect) exist primarily to make other editions look like better values by comparison
money_quotes: The price of software has very little to do with cost, and everything to do with perceived value.
Never compete on price when you don't have a price advantage. If you're the small company, you don't have the price advantage.
Consumers don't really know how to judge the value of software so well, so they grope around for nearby products to use as baselines.

IPSwitch was hoping their ISP customers, who have never remotely considered using Exchange due to the extremely high per-user cost, would compare their new price to Exchange, but of course, what they really compared it to was the price of the old version of their product.
They underestimated how much more Microsoft can charge for the same junk simply because Microsoft is the market leader.
My sources inform me that this was a decision made by the MBAs in the company over the strong objections of the developers. It's ironic that the MBAs don't even get the basic MBA stuff right.
key_insight: Software pricing is driven by perceived value relative to a baseline comparison, and companies that try to reframe their value equation by chasing a larger competitor's market will fail when customers compare the new price to the old one instead.
